title: The real and financial effects of internal liquidity: Evidence from the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act
abstract: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act unlocked as much as $1.7 trillion of U.S. multinationalsвҖҷ foreign cash. We examine the real and financial response to this liquidity shock and find that firms did not increase capital expenditures, employment, R&D, or M&A, regardless of financial constraints. On the financial side, firms paid out only about one-third of the new liquidity to shareholders and retained half as cash. This high retention was not associated with poor governance. The high propensity to retain the liquidity shock as cash, even among well-governed firms with limited financial constraints, is difficult to reconcile with existing theory.

article.1. Data: To understand firmsвҖҷ response to a liquidity shock provided by new access to their foreign cash, we require high quality non-consolidated data on firmsвҖҷ domestic and foreign operations and financial positions. Unfortunately, such data are not publicly available. For example, data in CompustatвҖҷs geographic segment file are reported inconsistently across firms and are usually limited to information on sales. To address the shortcoming of publicly available data, one of the authors applied for and was granted access to the confidential income statements and balance sheets of U.S. multinationalsвҖҷ domestic and foreign operations, maintained on secure government servers at the Bureau of Economic Analysis.We construct our dataset from BEAвҖҷs annual and benchmark surveys of the Activities of U.S. Multinational Enterprises.11 The surveys collect separate information on both the domestic and foreign operations of U.S. multinationals and are conducted pursuant to the International Investment and Trade in Services Survey Act (hereafter the Act). The Act stipulates that the вҖңuse of an individual companyвҖҷs data for tax, investigative, or regulatory purposes is prohibitedвҖқ. Willful noncompliance with the Act may result in imprisonment for up to one year. For these reasons, in addition to their monitoring of corporate events and a system of internal data integrity checks, BEA believes the surveys accurately capture virtually complete data on the universe of U.S. direct investment abroad.We limit the sample to majority-owned affiliates, which are commonly referred to as вҖңsubsidiariesвҖқ, the term used in this paper. In addition, we omit firms in the financial services (NAICS 5200вҖ“5299), utility (NAICS 2200-2299), and public administration (NAICS 9200-9299) industries. We also require that firms have at least one subsidiary in a country with a corporate income tax rate below the U.S. rate prior to 2018. We merge these data with Compustat, SDC, and FactSet to incorporate information on stock repurchases, mergers and acquisitions, and institutional equity ownership, respectively. We start the sample in 2010 to include several years prior to the TCJA while avoiding the turbulence of the financial crisis. In the baseline analysis we end the sample in 2019, which is the last year for which BEAвҖҷs data are available. The baseline sample consists of 3146 firm-year observations, corresponding to 489 unique firms. For certain analyses that rely on consolidated (Compustat) outcomes, we extend the sample through 2021.In Table 1, we present descriptive statistics for the consolidated firms (Panel A), their domestic operations (Panel B), and their foreign operations (Panel C). We scale dollar-denominated stock variables (e.g., cash) by consolidated book value of assets and flow variables (e.g., dividends) by lagged consolidated book value of assets. Throughout the paper, financial variables are recorded in millions of 2018 U.S. dollars. In addition, we winsorize each variable at its 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate the influence of outliers. Appendix A contains detailed definitions for all variables.Panel A indicates that the ratio of consolidated cash to consolidated assets (Cash) averages 0.151. Sample firmsвҖҷ foreign subsidiaries tend to face relatively low tax rates. Specifically, the U.S. statutory tax rate in 2017 (35%) less the sales weighted average foreign statutory tax rate in 2017 (TaxGap) averages 10.5%. Panel B shows that domestic capital expenditures (U.S. investment) is roughly one fifth as large as domestic employment compensation (U.S. employment). Finally, Panel C indicates that foreign cash makes up about one-third of total cash: the ratio of foreign cash to consolidated assets in 2017 (Foreign cash) is 0.050, compared to a consolidated cash ratio of 0.151. Repatriations from a firmвҖҷs foreign subsidiaries to its U.S. parent, scaled by lagged consolidated assets, (Repatriations) averages 0.7% annually.Table 1. Descriptive statistics. article.2. Empirical design: We study a provision of the TCJA that eliminated the repatriation tax on U.S. multinational firmsвҖҷ accumulated foreign profits. With this change, firms suddenly had access to additional internal capital at zero marginal cost, funds that previously had been extremely costly to use for U.S. investment or payouts.12The TCJA, as legislation, emerged rapidly and was passed and implemented in short order. The earliest draft of the text was introduced in the U.S. House of Representatives on November 2, 2017. The final bill was signed into law on December 22, 2017, and became effective less than two months after it was introduced on January 1, 2018. Earlier in 2017 there was discussion of tax reform, but whether it would happen, when, and what would be included in a possible law (particularly, whether it would unlock firmsвҖҷ foreign cash) were unknown.13 Indeed, the details of the legislation were being negotiated even in the final hours before the vote in the Senate, with holdouts demanding changes that had to be handwritten in the margins of the bill to secure a slim 51-vote passage (Jansen, 2017; Pramuk, 2017). As a result, in conjunction with the fixed effects and control variables described below, we believe the TCJA generated a plausibly exogenous shock to firmsвҖҷ internal liquidity.To estimate the response in real and financial variables following the TCJA liquidity shock, we implement a difference-in-differences research design. Our baseline specification is (1)ЙӣYi,t=ОІГ—TCJAtГ—ForeignCashi+ОівҖІXi,tвҲ’1+Оҙj,t+Оұi+Йӣi,t,where Yi,t is the outcome variable of interest, such as investment, payouts, and cash holdings, for firm i in year t. The treatment intensity (ForeignCashi) is the firmвҖҷs foreign cash holdings scaled by total firm assets in 2017, our proxy for the amount of liquidity unlocked by the TCJA.14 The indicator TCJAt equals one after the TCJA was implemented (i.e., in 2018 and later), and is zero in other years.Critically, our baseline specification includes firm fixed effects (Оұi). These remove unobserved firm-level heterogeneity that may be correlated with foreign cash accumulation and drive investment decisions. In addition, industry-by-year fixed effects (Оҙj,t), where industry is categorized by four-digit NAICS codes, help control for potential industry-specific responses to the implementation of the TCJA.Since one of our primary goals is to identify the effect of liquidity increases on real investment, we must ensure that our treatment intensity measure does not act as a proxy for unobserved investment opportunities. Firm and industry-by-year fixed effects control for both (1) time-invariant cross-sectional differences in investment opportunities, and (2) industry-average changes in investment opportunities over time, including those brought about by the TCJA. These controls largely address several concerns, including that the TCJA included a tax rate cut on U.S. income, potentially improving after-tax investment returns for all firms. Still, there may be a firm-specific change in investment opportunities brought about by the TCJA. If (and only if) this firm-specific change is correlated with pre-TCJA foreign cash accumulation, and is omitted from the specification, then this could bias our estimates.It is worth noting that this omitted variable bias would generally overstate the response of investment to the liquidity increase because foreign cash is likely to be positively correlated with the firm-specific increase in foreign investment opportunities. For example, a firm that generates most of its income in a low-tax country would benefit more from the removal of the repatriation tax provided by the TCJA.15Nevertheless, to address this concern, we construct a control for the firm-specific change in investment opportunities generated by the TCJA based on the source of each firmвҖҷs foreign income. In particular, we define the firmвҖҷs TaxGap as the U.S. statutory corporate tax rate minus the average statutory tax rate faced by the firmвҖҷs subsidiaries, where the average is weighted by subsidiary sales, constructed using 2017 data. Because the measure is intended to capture the change in post-reform investment opportunities, the tax gap control is set to zero prior to 2018. That is, we use TCJAГ—TaxGap in the regression specifications.16 The tax gap measures the reduction in the repatriation tax rate brought about by the TCJA: a higher value corresponds to a larger increase in after-tax returns on future foreign investment. Thus, the tax gap measure is likely to capture firm-specific changes in investment opportunities that may be associated with the TCJA.17 We use statutory tax rates in the calculation, as effective tax rates are likely influenced by firmsвҖҷ choices (Dharmapala, 2014).Our baseline regressions (Tables 3, 4, and 11), and many additional analyses, include this TCJAГ—TaxGap control, as well as a control for firm age. The latter is not directly chosen by the firm, at least on an ongoing basis, and is therefore arguably exogenous to the outcomes we study. Partly for this reason, it commonly appears in the literature (e.g., Giroud, 2013). We define age as the logarithm of 1 plus the number of years since the firm first appears in the Compustat data.In the regressions with real outcomes, we add additional controls that often appear in the empirical literature analysing real economic activity. Specifically, we include controls for the lagged market-to-book ratio (Dharmapala et al., 2011; Almeida and Campello, 2007; Graham et al., 2017) and lagged firm profitability (Shin and Stulz, 1998; Badertscher et al., 2013; Shroff et al., 2014).We are also sympathetic to the view that controls may be correlated with both our treatment and Yi,t, leading to biased estimates of the treatment effect (Gormley and Matsa, 2011; Roberts and Whited, 2013). Accordingly, we re-estimate our main specifications omitting all controls. We discuss the results, which are qualitatively and quantitatively similar to the baseline, in Section 3.Our treatment variable, ForeignCash, uses unconsolidated financial data from BEA to measure the cash and marketable securities held in the foreign subsidiaries of U.S. firms in 2017. This measure provides advantages in our setting relative to alternative measures. One measure commonly used in the literature is the foreign income that the firm chooses to designate, for accounting purposes, as вҖңpermanently reinvested earningsвҖқ (PRE), which may be held as financial assets or invested in real assets.18 PRE includes substantial real investments that will not increase firm liquidity following the TCJA. In addition, foreign profits that are not designated as PRE can still be held in foreign subsidiaries, as cash or real assets. In contrast, our measure ensures that we are capturing cash and financial assets, rather than illiquid real investments, that truly enhance a firmвҖҷs liquidity position, regardless of the firmвҖҷs chosen PRE accounting designation.19For most outcome variables, we also explore the dynamics of the treatment effect by estimating the response using year-by-year indicators in lieu of the single TCJAt indicator: (2)ЙӣYi,t=вҲ‘sвҲҲTОІsГ—1t=sГ—ForeignCashi+ОівҖІXi,tвҲ’1+Оҙj,t+Оұi+Йӣi,t,where T={2010вҲ’2014,2015,2016,2018,2019}, that is, a five-year pre-period plus the remaining years in our sample excluding 2017 (the reference year).
article.3. Results: In this section, we first estimate the effect of the TCJA-induced increase in internal liquidity on firmвҖҷs real investment decisions, including capital expenditures, employment, research and development (R&D), and mergers and acquisitions (M&A). We consider whether financial constraints or agency conflicts play a role in this response. Next, we explore the response in the financial positions of firms following the unlocking of their foreign cash, including changes in payouts, cash holdings, and leverage.Before proceeding with our main empirical tests, we first explore whether our treatment variableвҖ”a firmвҖҷs foreign cash ratioвҖ”predicts subsidiary-to-parent repatriations. Following the TCJA, the distinction between foreign and domestic cash was effectively nullified for U.S. tax purposes, meaning that a firm could pay dividends from its foreign subsidiaries to its U.S. parent (i.e., a repatriation) without tax consequences in the U.S. If firms were holding cash in their foreign subsidiaries due to the tax lockout created by repatriation taxes prior to 2018, we might expect to see foreign cash holdings partially reallocated to U.S. parents.Table 2 shows the results of our baseline regression using Eq. (1) with subsidiary-to-parent repatriations as the outcome variable. Column (1) shows that 13 cents of each dollar of foreign cash was repatriated annually in the two years following the reform, meaning roughly one quarter of the foreign cash was repatriated. The year-by-year estimates, using Eq. (2), are reported in Column (2), indicating that considerable repatriation occurs each year post-TCJA. The increase in repatriations following reform can also clearly be seen in the unconditional dollar amount of repatriations shown in Fig. 1.20The reallocation of cash from foreign subsidiaries to U.S. parents provides suggestive evidence that taxes played a role in firmsвҖҷ decision to accumulate foreign cash. Real economic activityDid removing the tax lockout on foreign cash stimulate investment and employment by U.S. multinationals? We assess the effect of the increased liquidity on firmsвҖҷ real activity by estimating the difference-in-differences specification in Eq. (1). Table 3 reports the results for domestic (U.S.) investment and employment. We define investment as capital expenditures and employment as wage expense, each scaled by lagged assets.Columns (1) and (3) report the results for the average treatment effect in the two-year post period. Across both measures of U.S. real activity, we find no significant response by firms to the increased access to foreign cash. Contrary to the hopes of some TCJA proponents, these results indicate that the reform likely did not have a discernible effect on the real decisions of firms in our sample. Financial constraints, agency conflicts, and profit shiftingWe find that firms do not respond to increased access to liquidity by increasing capital expenditures, employment, or R&D. However, basic financial theory predicts that investment would increase if the firm is financially constrained (Stein, 1997; Giroud and Mueller, 2015), whereby an increase in internal capital would lower the cost of capital. In contrast, investment would not increase for unconstrained firms. We explore this possibility by testing whether the investment response depends on financial constraints.Financial constraints are notoriously difficult to measure (Farre-Mensa and Ljungqvist, 2016). With this caveat, we rely on the measure of Hadlock and Pierce (2010) based on firm size and age. We augment the baseline regression in Eq. (1) to include the interaction with financial constraints, specifically: (3)ЙӣYi,t=ОІ0Г—TCJAtГ—ForeignCashiГ—HPi+ОІ1Г—TCJAtГ—ForeignCashi+ОІ2Г—TCJAtГ—HPi+ОівҖІXi,t+Оҙj,t+Оұi+Йӣi,t HP is the Hadlock and Pierce (2010) measure of financial constraints. A positive estimate for ОІ0 would indicate that more financially constrained firms increase investment relative to unconstrained firms in response to the freeing of foreign cash.Table 6 reports the results from estimating Eq. (3). For all outcome variables, both in the U.S. and overseas, the estimated coefficient on the interaction is insignificant. Overall, we find no evidence that financial constraints are a significant predictor of investment response to the TCJA liquidity shock. These findings further support our previous results indicating that U.S. multinationals are not significantly constrained. This is consistent with the findings of Dharmapala et al. (2011) for U.S. multinationals, and with Farre-Mensa et al. (2022) for the universe of Compustat firms.In addition to financial constraints, misaligned incentives between managers and shareholders may lead to an increase in real activity in response to the liquidity spike even if the firm is not financially constrained. We consider this possibility by repeating the estimation in Eq. (3) using a proxy for the strength of corporate governance in the place of financial constraints. We proxy for corporate governance using the fraction of equity ownership of the top 5 institutional investors, as defined by Ferreira and Matos (2008). The results are reported in Table 7. For U.S. investment, U.S. employment, and foreign employment, the interaction term is insignificant, suggesting that agency conflicts are not an important driver of real activity in response to increased access to cash. For foreign investment, an outcome with no clear prediction, the interaction term is statistically significant at the 10% level, but is economically small.22Table 6. Mergers and acquisitionsWe next consider another form of real economic activity: acquisitions. For reasons similar to those for other real outcomes, increased access to liquidity may lead to increased acquisition activity. This could include decreased cost of capital, as well as exacerbated agency conflicts such as empire building or free cash flow problems (Jensen, 1986; Harford, 1999). Relatedly, Hanlon et al. (2015) finds that trapped foreign cash led to an increase in foreign acquisitions made with cash, and that these acquisitions had lower announcement returns.We test whether increased access to cash led to an increase in acquisition activity by estimating our baseline Eq. (1) with the dollar volume of acquisitions, scaled by lagged assets, as the outcome variable. The results are reported in Table 9. Similar to Hanlon et al. (2015), we focus on acquisitions made with cash. Consistent with other forms of real activity, we find no effect of increased access to liquidity on firm M&A activity. This is true for both U.S. acquisitions, which are most likely to be affected by the reform, as well as foreign acquisitions. These results contrast with other work that indicates both acquisitions and announcement returns are increasing in foreign cash (see Atwood et al., 2020 and Amberger and Robinson, 2022, respectively). In contrast to existing work, this study uses foreign cash data directly observed by BEA, potentially contributing to the different findings.In addition to a reduced cost of capital, agency conflictsвҖ”such as empire building motivesвҖ”may lead managers to engage in increased acquisition activity. We consider the role of agency conflicts by including an interaction with our measure of the strength of corporate governance, the equity ownership of the top-5 institutional investors. The results are reported in Table 10. For both U.S. and foreign acquisition activity, we find no evidence that managers facing less institutional oversight responded to the liquidity shock by increasing acquisition activity.Table 9. Cash holdings, shareholder payouts, and leverageIn the previous section, we found that firms did not adjust their real economic activity in response to increased liquidity following the TCJA. In this section, we turn to the financial decisions of the firm. How did firms adjust their financial positions in response to a shock to internal liquidity?To explore how firms responded to the freeing of their foreign cash, we estimate Eqs. (1), (2) for several financial outcome variables. The results are reported in Table 11. Column (2) shows the dynamics of the consolidated cash holdings as a function of 2017 foreign cash (ForeignCash); both variables are scaled by total assets. As expected, firms with higher 2017 foreign cash experience a larger run up in consolidated cash prior to 2017, as indicated by the positive trend in the estimated coefficients during the pre-period.23 Following the reform, consolidated cash holdings begin to decline more for those firms with more foreign cash in 2017. For each dollar of foreign cash that was freed by the TCJA, firms reduced consolidated cash holdings by 48 cents two years after the reform. Thus, firms adjusted their consolidated cash positions downward in proportion to the liquidity shock induced by the TCJA. This suggests that at least some of the foreign cash holdings were held for tax purposes, consistent with Foley et al. (2007), Graham and Leary (2018), and Faulkender et al. (2019). It also provides additional support for the assertion that the recent upward trend in corporate cash holdings is linked to the worldwide tax system.The dynamic estimates in Column (2) indicate that firms that accumulated foreign cash also reduced consolidated cash following the reform (see also Panel E of Fig. 2). Column (1) reports the single point estimate comparing consolidated cash holdings in the period 2010вҖ“17 versus 2018вҖ“19. Consistent with the pattern of the dynamic estimates, the point estimate is insignificant, although negative, indicating that the post-reform decline in consolidated cash holdings represents an unwinding of the pre-reform accumulation of foreign cash.Table 11. Columns (3)вҖ“(8) of Table 11 report point and dynamic estimates for dividends, share repurchases, and their sum (total payouts). Share repurchases significantly increase in response to the access to foreign cash, while dividends are unchanged.24 This is consistent with prior evidence that repurchases (dividends) are more likely used for transitory (persistent) changes to shareholder payouts (Jagannathan et al., 2000; Guay and Harford, 2000). However, the magnitude of the response in repurchases is relatively modest, at 15 cents per year, for two years, for each marginal dollar of foreign cash. Although modest in magnitude, payouts can account for a substantial fraction of the reduction in consolidated cash holdings.25To assess the robustness of these results, we repeat the estimates of the dynamic effects for consolidated cash holdings, repurchases, and total payouts, excluding all control variables. The results are reported in Online Appendix Table OA.2. We also explore including the firmsвҖҷ effective tax rates as an additional control variable, reported in Table OA.3. In both cases, the results are very similar.Our findings on payouts have a notable connection to the public economics literature on dividend taxes. The TCJA reduced the tax cost of accessing foreign cash, including for firms intending to make distributions to shareholders. In this sense, the policy change had an effect similar to a reduction in the dividend tax rate that was enacted as part of the Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003 (JGTRRA).26 Chetty and Saez (2005) and Yagan (2015) find that the JGTRRA dividend tax cut is associated with increased dividend payments. Consistent with this result, we find that the reduced tax cost of repatriating foreign cash was associated with an increase in payouts. Furthermore, Yagan (2015) finds that the JGTRRA dividend tax cut had no significant effect on firmsвҖҷ investment and employee compensation. As discussed in Section 3.1, we also find the reduction in the tax cost to firms of accessing their foreign cash had no impact on firmsвҖҷ investment or employment decisions. These complementary results reinforce the notion that dividend taxes impact payout policy, but have minimal effect on firmsвҖҷ real activity.We also consider whether the foreign cash freed by the TCJA was used to pay down debt and reduce leverage. Prior to the TCJA, firms may have used their trapped foreign cash to support borrowing in the U.S.вҖ”financing that could be used for U.S. payouts and investment without restrictionвҖ”as a way to circumvent the repatriation tax and indirectly pay out foreign profits to U.S. shareholders (De Simone and Lester, 2018). A post-reform decline in debt could reflect an unwinding of those pre-TCJA tax-arbitrage positions. However, Columns (9) and (10) of Table 11 show no significant change in book leverage following the TCJA. This result suggests that firms did not use their freed foreign cash to adjust their debt positions.27We next consider three factors that may affect a firmвҖҷs financial response to the freeing of its foreign cash. First, we consider whether foreign cash was being held for operational reasons rather than purely for tax reasons. We test this possibility by measuring the TCJA-induced liquidity increase as the portion of foreign cash that is in excess of that required to support foreign operations. Excess foreign cash is determined in four steps. First, the ratio of cash to net property, plant, and equipment is calculated for domestic only Compustat firms (i.e., non-multinationals) in 2017. Second, the median of this ratio within 2-digit NAICS industry bins is matched to each foreign subsidiary and multiplied by the subsidiaryвҖҷs net property, plant, and equipment, to obtain a predicted cash value. Third, this predicted cash value is subtracted from the subsidiaryвҖҷs cash holdings. If the difference is negative it is set to zero. Fourth, these excess cash values are aggregated across a firmвҖҷs foreign subsidiaries and scaled by consolidated assets. We denote this variable ExForCash.We measure the response in consolidated cash holdings, repurchases, and payouts, by estimating Eq. (2) using the firmsвҖҷ 2017 level of excess foreign cash in place of total foreign cash. Table 12 reports the results. For all three outcome variables, the coefficient estimates using excess foreign cash are very similar to our baseline results. This suggests that the muted response in payouts and consolidated cash holdings was unlikely due to the need to keep the freed cash in the foreign subsidiaries for operational reasons.Second, managers may receive private benefits from the firmвҖҷs cash holdings and therefore choose to retain more cash than is optimal from the perspective of shareholders. Indeed, Harford et al. (2017) finds that the value of a firmвҖҷs foreign cash holdings is discounted by its shareholders, and Kim et al. (2023) finds that shareholders responded positively to a policy that pressured Korean firms to disgorge excess cash. To test whether agency conflicts are associated with higher retention of freed cash, we re-estimate the financial response with the inclusion of an interaction with a proxy for corporate governance. As institutional owners are likely able to exert pressure on management to pay out excess cash, we again use the top-5 institutional ownership of firms as a proxy for strength of governance. Table 13 reports the results of the estimation. In all cases, there is no differential effect attributable to institutional presence, providing no evidence that retention of cash is due to agency conflicts.Table 12. Excess foreign cash. Indeed, conventional wisdom and existing empirical evidence suggest that unconstrained firms would likely respond to the liquidity shock by paying out the excess liquidity to shareholders rather than retaining it as cash (Almeida et al., 2004, Dharmapala et al., 2011; Dharmapala, 2024).In contrast, our results suggest that firms do not quickly adjust cash ratios in response to positive liquidity shocks, similar to what Graham and Leary (2018) describes as вҖңlooseвҖқ adherence to cash targets. In other words, nominal cash holdings appear to be somewhat sticky, analogous to the nominal stickiness of debt documented by Welch (2004). One possibility is that this loose cash targeting is the result of fixed costs of adjustment. However, given that paying out excess cash is unlikely to be particularly costly, an alternative explanation is that firms may be roughly indifferent between paying out and retaining the increased liquidity. Specifically, prior to reform, the marginal benefits of retaining cash were high due to repatriation taxes, leading to foreign cash accumulation. After reform, only the вҖңtraditionalвҖқ cash holdings trade-off remains, that is, costs of carry and agency versus precautionary savings motives (e.g., Nikolov and Whited, 2014). For our sample of firms that appear to be well-governed and financially unconstrained, these traditional marginal costs and benefits are each likely quite small, leading to optimal cash holdings that may be roughly indeterminate. Simply put, firms had a strong tax incentive to accumulate cash prior to reform, but little compelling reason to adjust cash downward once the repatriation tax was removed by the TCJA.It is worth noting that firms did not reduce leverage in conjunction with their high retention of cash after the reform. Gao et al. (2021) show that it may be optimal for firms to hold cash alongside their debt positions because it reduces the likelihood of default and acts as a type of collateral, thus lowering the cost of borrowing. Their model extends the traditional precautionary savings model, and may help to explain why firms did not pay out more of their freed foreign cash.It is also possible that the optimal level of internal liquidityвҖ”represented by U.S. cash before the reform and consolidated cash afterвҖ”increased as a result of the TCJA. Specifically, the reduction in the U.S. corporate tax rate, which affected all firms in our sample, likely reduced the tax cost of holding cash inside the firm.28 Reducing the marginal cost of holding cash would, all else equal, increase the optimal level of cash holdings, which is consistent with our results. This implies that the marginal benefits of cash need not be large for firms to choose to retain freed cash.
article.4. Conclusion: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act unlocked up to $1.7 trillion in cash trapped in U.S. multinational firmsвҖҷ foreign operations. We construct a direct measure of firmsвҖҷ exposure to this policy change using detailed data from the Bureau of Economic Analysis and implement a difference-in-differences research design to explore its impact on their real and financial policies in the U.S. and abroad. We find no significant association between the policy change and firmsвҖҷ capital expenditures, employment, research and development, or mergers and acquisitions at either their domestic or foreign operations. These findings do not vary with the degree of firmsвҖҷ financial constraints. In terms of financial outcomes, the policy shock is not associated with firmsвҖҷ dividend or leverage decisions, but has a moderate positive association with stock repurchases. Firms with more trapped liquidity pre-TCJA also reduce their consolidated cash holdings post-TCJA by 48 cents for each dollar of foreign cash unlocked. Firms thus retain about half of the freed cash. Strength of corporate governance does not appear to mediate firmsвҖҷ financial responses to the policy change. Our findings suggest that increased access to capital likely did not have a significant effect on firmsвҖҷ real decisions, and demonstrates only a loose targeting of cash ratios. This is consistent with small marginal costs and benefits of holding cash for these firms, as well as a limited incentive for firms to pay out excess cash accumulated overseas.
